coredata.dc:title: Recent development of quantum dots@metal-organic framework composites as potential chemical and biological luminescence nanosensors
coredata.dc:creator: Balakrishnan, Thivyah
Ang, Wei Lun
Mahmoudi, Ebrahim
Sambudi, Nonni Soraya
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence-based sensors have gained considerable attention in the chemical and biological fields, owing to their numerous advantages, including high sensitivity and selectivity, ease of operation, wide linear detection range, and rapid analysis. While various types of quantum dots (QDs) with remarkable optical properties have been extensively explored as emerging fluorescence sensing materials, persistent efforts are dedicated to enhancing QD dispersibility and stability. To address this challenge, the concept of encapsulating QDs within metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) has been introduced and advanced. MOFs, a subclass of porous coordination material with excellent features like high porosity, large specific surface areas, and chemically tunable pore surface, enable the fabrication of QD@MOF nanocomposites with improved stability and enhanced chemical and biological sensing in terms of sensitivity and response range. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the latest developments involving QD@MOF materials, encompassing various luminescence origins, current synthesis methodologies, and the evolving advancements of these nanocomposites in the context of chemical and biological sensing. The review places specific emphasis on receptor and response types, as well as the underlying detection mechanisms, offering valuable insights into the effective design of QD@MOF as a fluorescent sensor. Finally, the paper outlines the challenges and future prospects in this burgeoning research area.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Metal-organic frameworks
Luminescence
Detection mechanisms
Chemical and biological sensing

head.ce:title: Recent development of quantum dots@metal-organic framework composites as potential chemical and biological luminescence nanosensors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence-based sensors have gained considerable attention in the chemical and biological fields, owing to their numerous advantages, including high sensitivity and selectivity, ease of operation, wide linear detection range, and rapid analysis. While various types of quantum dots (QDs) with remarkable optical properties have been extensively explored as emerging fluorescence sensing materials, persistent efforts are dedicated to enhancing QD dispersibility and stability. To address this challenge, the concept of encapsulating QDs within metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) has been introduced and advanced. MOFs, a subclass of porous coordination material with excellent features like high porosity, large specific surface areas, and chemically tunable pore surface, enable the fabrication of QD@MOF nanocomposites with improved stability and enhanced chemical and biological sensing in terms of sensitivity and response range. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the latest developments involving QD@MOF materials, encompassing various luminescence origins, current synthesis methodologies, and the evolving advancements of these nanocomposites in the context of chemical and biological sensing. The review places specific emphasis on receptor and response types, as well as the underlying detection mechanisms, offering valuable insights into the effective design of QD@MOF as a fluorescent sensor. Finally, the paper outlines the challenges and future prospects in this burgeoning research area.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past two decades, highly luminescent quantum dots (QDs) including inorganic semiconductor QDs, carbon-based QDs, and perovskite QDs have been actively investigated and applied in various chemical and biological applications due to their unique optical and electrochemical properties such as excellent photoluminescence (PL), tunable, and size-dependent electronic properties. Compared to conventional fluorophores, QDs offer several advantages, including broad absorption bands, symmetrical and narrow emission bands, low photobleaching, increased photostability, long lifetime, and high quantum yield [1–5]. These exceptional qualities have made QDs potential alternatives for numerous applications, including chemical sensing, cellular imaging, nanomedicine, photocatalysis, optoelectronics, and energy conversion. One of the most prominent applications of QDs is their use as fluorescent sensors in both chemical and biological systems. However, despite their potential for various applications, ongoing efforts are dedicated to improving the dispersibility and stability of QDs. The tendency for QDs to aggregate in high concentrations leads to poor dispersibility and fluorescence quenching [6]. This fluorescence quenching, resulting from QDs aggregation, is one of the primary drawbacks in their applications, which can significantly reduce their PL quantum yields. Additionally, they are sensitive to light and temperature and exhibit weak chemical stability in real environmental conditions [7]. Traditional strategies for assembling QDs with enhanced stability and dispersibility typically involve altering the nature of surfactants through surface ligand exchange or utilizing encapsulation techniques to improve surface functionalization [1,8]. However, ligand exchange often causes fluorescence quenching of QDs, primarily due to the development of trapped states during the exchange process. On the other hand, controlled encapsulation of QDs in various media, such as polymers or other porous materials, can prevent QD aggregation and provide long-term stability [9–11]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore ways to incorporate these nanoparticles into designable and tunable hybrid matrices to confer multifunctional properties upon QDs.

To overcome the challenges described above, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer interesting advantages. MOFs, a subclass of porous coordination material, have inspired extensive studies aimed at developing novel technologies. These highly crystalline structures feature an organic-inorganic hybrid nature resulting from the self-assembly of coordination bonds using organic ligands as linking units and metal clusters as nodes [12,13]. MOFs possess unique characteristics, including high porosity, large specific surface areas, chemically tunable pore surfaces, and satisfactory thermal stability, which make them promising candidates for various applications in the chemical and biological fields [14–17]. Moreover, thanks to their excellent reversibility, rapid sorption kinetics, and the ability to respond to external influences, various types of MOFs are now employed as sensing probes for specific analytes, including metal ions [18,19], toxic gases [20–22], nitroaromatic explosives [23–25], anions and bio-chemicals [26,27], humidity [28,29], and temperature [30,31]. Consequently, the exceptional physiochemical properties of MOFs render them ideal hosts for immobilizing luminescent quantum dots (QDs).

The QDs embedded in MOFs, hereafter referred to as QD@MOF, can be classified into three groups based on the encapsulation pattern of the QDs within the MOFs. This classification takes into account the degree of matching between the size of the QDs and the pore size of the MOFs [32,33]. In the first category, the QDs are enclosed within the pores of the MOFs, allowing them to be orderly immobilized within the MOF framework without agglomeration, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. In the second category, the QDs are primarily anchored on the outer surface of the MOFs, as depicted in Fig. 1b. In this configuration, the unrestricted growth of QDs on the MOFs' surface can lead to aggregation, resulting in the poor homogeneity of the composites. In the third configuration, some of the QDs are enclosed within the pores of the MOFs, while others are deposited on the surface of the MOFs, as shown in Fig. 1c. This phenomenon may be due to an excess of QDs compared to the available pores in the MOFs, causing them to attach to the MOFs' surface. Another possible reason is the larger size of certain QDs, preventing them from entering the MOFs' pores and resulting in their deposition on the outer surface instead.

The incorporation of ultra-high luminescence QDs and hybrid behaviors of MOFs for synthesis of QD@MOF nanocomposites is expected to be a promising solution in developing a new family of luminescence nanosensor that would outperform the other traditional sensory materials. Some of the remarkable enhancements that make them desirable for sensing applications are [2,16,34–37]:
i)
The geometrical structure of MOFs can prevent aggregation and leaching of embedded QDs, thus improving its stability even under harsh conditions.
ii)
The great adsorption performance by most of the MOFs enables accumulation of target analytes on their active sites inside the pores, resulting in high detection sensitivity and selectivity.
iii)
The QD@MOF nanocomposites can also act as molecular sieves, which means only the target analytes with an appropriate molecular size are allowed to diffuse into the framework and be detected, thus enhancing the selectivity of detection.
iv)
The incorporation of QDs with luminous MOFs enables ratiometric fluorescence sensing of multiple analytes, leading to self-calibration, thereby eliminating the interference of the experimental environment.
v)
The highly ordered crystalline framework of QD@MOF enables repetitive interaction between the framework and the target analyte, resulting in repeatability of the detection response.
vi)
In addition to the fluorescence generated by the QDs and MOFs, the presence of luminescent guest molecules, including dyes and metal nanoclusters, also contributes significantly to the development of a multi-luminescent QD@MOF nanocomposite. Incorporating additional emissive components into the QD@MOF nanocomposite further boosts its fluorescence capabilities, enabling precise and specific detection of target analytes.
vii)
The fluorescence sensing methods are highly sensitive with very low limits of detection, easy to manipulate, practical in on-site applications, have fast response time, good reproducibility and can be directly addressable through powdered materials. A crucial benefit of PL methods is that either powder or crystals of QD@MOF are applicable for chemical and biological sensing without the need for film fabrication. The synergistic effects between MOFs and QDs would lead to advancement of cost effectiveness and efficiency, making QD@MOF composites promising as sensory materials.

Up to now, extensive reviews have been conducted separately on QDs [38–44] and luminescent MOFs [45–52] in their respective applications. While each of these reviews is useful and practical, few have focused on strategies to enhance the performance of QDs by embedding the QDs into the matrix of MOFs. Given the enormous interest in QD@MOF composites, the aim of this review is to highlight the synergistic potential when these two materials are combined by examining the synthetic strategies of luminous QD@MOF and the latest developments in the performance of QD@MOF-based luminescent sensor for detection of various analytes such as cations, anions, nitroaromatics, water, and temperature. This is followed by an overview on the communication between signal generation with the receptor and response types, and detection mechanism in QD@MOF to gain more insight into its design and utilization as an effective fluorescent sensor. The enhanced performance of QD@MOF nanocomposites for various applications is then discussed. Finally, the review sheds light on this less explored area by discussing some of the limitations that laboratory-scale investigations still need to address, while highlighting the potential of luminescent QD@MOF nanocomposites for industrial applications in the near future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General strategies to construct QD@MOF composite
ce:section.ce:para: To develop an ideal QD@MOF optical nanosensor for various sensing applications, it is crucial to construct QD@MOF nanocomposite while retaining the original features and configurations of the QDs and MOFs. The interaction of QDs with organic linkers and metal nodes of MOFs through π to π forces or coordination bonds would lead to the regulation of the electronic properties of the QDs and as a result, their activity is modified. Taking into consideration the spatial distribution, nanosize of QDs, and guest-host interactions, researchers have reported several simple approaches to design highly versatile QD@MOF hybrids [8,34,35]. To successfully encapsulate QDs within MOFs, five well-established techniques have been used; these are a) ship-in-bottle, b) bottle-around-ship, c) sandwich assembly, d) in situ encapsulation, and e) direct loading, as shown in Fig. 2 .

The construction of QDs in a preformed MOF constitutes the ship-in-bottle technique (Fig. 2a). Typically, the porous MOFs are synthesized and added into the solution containing the QD precursors, and they are then allowed to react under optimal QD development conditions. For instance, Lin et al. successfully developed MIL-53(Fe)/CQD by mixing the presynthesized MIL-53(Fe) particles with glucose solution and heating the mixture to 200 °C until the glucose molecules were carbonized to CQDs inside the MIL-53(Fe) cavities [53].

By using this method, the synthesis processes of MOFs and QDs do not interfere with each other, which is beneficial for the preparation of QD@MOF nanocomposites with retained crystallinity and fluorescence. This technique creates a chemically simple interface between the QDs and MOFs. Unfortunately, some key obstacles need to be addressed. i) The MOFs must be able to withstand the conditions needed to synthesize the QDs. ii) The structure of the MOFs can be partially degraded if the QDs are bigger than the pore diameter of the presynthesized MOFs. Additionally, the larger QDs might aggregate externally on the surface of the MOFs, which can result in unpredictable spatial distribution in the QD@MOF nanocomposite. Furthermore, the agglomerations decrease the surface area of MOFs as it blocks the pores of the MOFs [34]. iii) The challenge remains to precisely control the structure, composition, shape and internal location of the QDs within the matrix of the MOFs [8].

The MOFs are arranged around the presynthesized QDs in the bottle-around-ship approach (Fig. 2b). Generally, this method involves two steps; first, the QDs with uniform size and shape are formed and often stabilized by the capping agents or surfactants, and second, the synthesized QDs are added into the precursor solutions of MOFs and allowed to react under similar conditions required to produce the pure MOFs. The use of capping agents or surfactants prevents the aggregation of QDs and enhances the overgrowth of the shell of the MOFs on the presynthesized QDs. Asadi et al. successfully constructed fluorescent PEG-ZnS QD@ZIF-67 nanocrystals via the bottle-around-ship method [54]. The PEG capped ZnS QDs were prepared and then mixed with 2-methylimidazole and Co(NO3)2·6H2O methanol solutions (ZIF-67 precursor solutions) under stirring for 45 min at room temperature to form the desired nanocomposite. In contrast, Saha et al. reported a capping-agent-free method to immobilize QDs within MOFs using the bottle-around-ship strategy [55]. The CdS QDs without any capping were encapsulated in a low-molecular-weight metallohydrogel (ZAVA), and then directly transformed into a CdS-loaded MOF (CdS@ZAVCl) upon addition of sodium chloride. Since the methodology did not involve a capping agent, the QDs were able to retain their luminescence and were well exposed after encapsulation inside the MOF crystals.

The QD@MOF nanocomposite prepared by bottle-around-ship strategy possesses several advantages compared to the previously discussed technique. Controllable size, morphology, and well-defined structure of embedded QDs can be easily achieved due to their preassembly prior to the formation of the MOFs' shell. This method is also the most preferred and used among all methods because of its simplicity and ease of synthesis. However, the increased energy barrier between the QDs and the MOFs would cause difficulties in subsequent growth of MOFs on the presynthesized QDs [34]. Moreover, the introduction of capping agents or the presence of metal ions (MOFs’ precursor) might lead to partial PL quenching of the QDs and may prevent complete exposure of active sites [16]. To overcome this challenge, it is necessary to carefully select the MOFs and QDs and rationally design the QD@MOF nanocomposite to ensure the properties of both the materials are preserved.

The QD@MOF nanocomposite constructed by sandwich assembly strategy generally consists of sandwich-like layer-by-layer arrangement that involves 3 steps [34]: i) the MOF core synthesis, ii) presynthesized QDs deposition, and iii) the sequential growth of the MOF shell on the MOF core, as illustrated in Fig. 2c. In addition, the repetition of QD deposition and MOF overgrowth would result in multi-layered QD@MOF nanocomposite with tunable thickness and controllable structures. For example, single-shell CQDs@ZIF-82 nanocrystals have been successfully prepared through the double-solvent method, in which the surface of the CQDs@ZIF-8 was coated by pure ZIF-8 shell [56]. The CQDs@ZIF-82 displayed the strongest blue PL emission compared to CQDs solution and featured the same truncated cube shape as the CQDs@ZIF-8 but was larger. This study established that the QD@MOF crystal quality in terms of shape and optical characteristics is not diminished by sequential growth and multi-shell structure. The sandwich assembly strategy enables the position, shape, and composition of embedded QDs to be precisely controlled. However, achieving fracture free and conformal overgrowth of MOF shells on the core remains a big challenge in this method. Two MOFs of similar net topologies are highly preferred to match the connectivity and site symmetries of the crystal structures of both the inner and external MOFs. Incorporation of two MOFs with different topologies can be performed by introducing cationic surfactant or capping agents to ensure a well matched and fracture free shell is constructed [57].

The “one-step” strategy involves the mixing of the precursor solution of QDs and MOFs, whereby the QDs and MOFs are synthesized simultaneously and assembled into the QD@MOF composites (Fig. 2d). Xin et al. encapsulated CDs into UiO-66(OH)2 to develop blue emissive CDs@UiO-66(OH)2 via “one step” synthetic method as described in Fig. 3 [58,59]. Zirconyl chloride octahydrate, tetraethylammonium bromide and 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid were mixed and heated at 180 °C for 24 h to form CDs@UiO-66(OH)2. By controlling several key synthesis parameters such as precursor, capping agent, temperature, and solvent, it is possible to optimize the crystallization and growth rates of the QDs and MOFs and successfully construct the desired QD@MOF nanocomposite via the “one step” method. Compared to other strategies that consist of stepwise procedures, this strategy is simple, straightforward, saves time and energy, and is low cost, making this method more environmentally friendly and efficient. Nevertheless, this “one-step” strategy is not widely used by researchers because of the difficulties in controlling the reaction conditions in synthesizing QDs and MOFs simultaneously and assembling both of them into a nanocomposite. Suitable experimental conditions are crucial to stabilize the in situ formed QDs and to promote the heteronucleation of MOFs on the interface of the QDs [35].

The direct loading method involves a separate synthesis of MOFs and QDs, and it is then combined to create QD@MOF nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 2e. Chen et al. recently synthesized a stable dual emissive UiO-66-NH2@CdTe complex through the direct loading approach [61]. They mixed a presynthesized UiO-66-NH2 solution and CdTe QDs solution in a ratio of 1:2 and incubated the mixed solution for 20 min at 35 °C to construct the UiO-66-NH2@CdTe composite. Similarly, Mousavi et al. successfully prepared MIL-101(Fe)@amine-CQDs nanocomposite by simply mixing amine-CQDs solution and MIL-101(Fe) for 12 h [62].

This strategy is beneficial in terms of maintaining the properties and original structures of the QDs and MOFs because the synthesis methodologies of QDs and MOFs do not interfere with one another. However, the size of QDs must be able to match the pore size of MOFs to successfully construct a QD@MOF composite with good qualities. QDs with larger size than the pore diameter of MOFs cannot be entrapped inside the cavities of the MOFs, thus restricting the formation of QD@MOF composites [1]. It is important to note that to create densely packed QDs in MOFs via the direct loading method, the size of QDs must correspond to the pore diameter of MOFs.

All the five strategies discussed above are beneficial for researchers to rationally construct the desired QD@MOF nanocomposites that could be potentially applied in multiple applications. These strategies have their own strengths and drawbacks as summarized in Table 1 . Further investigations on the development of QD@MOF nanocomposites with more clearly defined structures and more functionalities should be taken into consideration.

The fluorescence principle takes precedence in modifying the fluorescence characteristics of QD@MOFs for specific sensing. The PL emission generated by QD@MOF upon light irradiation in the absence of target analyte is the signal that will be utilized for sensing interpretation. Typically, the QD@MOFs are found to be a single emissive with the origin of fluorescence from the QDs. On the other hand, dual/multi emissive QD@MOFs have been extensively developed by incorporating various choices of QDs, MOFs and other luminous guests depending on their tunable PL characteristics [45,61]. It can be categorized into i) pure QD@MOF nanocomposite that has a singular PL emission, and ii) QD@MOF nanocomposite incorporated with other luminous species that give dual/multi-PL emissions as outlined in Fig. 4 and Table 2 .

As for the pure QD@MOF nanocomposite, the single fluorescence emission originates from the QDs that are embedded into the non-emissive MOFs. Meanwhile, the incorporation of QDs into fluorescent MOFs would form dual emissive QD@MOF nanocomposites, which are endowed with the fluorescence of both the QDs and MOFs. Fluorescent MOFs generally derive their emission source from the luminescent metal ions (such as lanthanide ions like Tb3+ and Eu3+) which serve as the coordination center, or it can be introduced through post synthetic modification [16,83]. For instance, in a study conducted by Ren et al., CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, and I) perovskite QDs with green fluorescence embedded into a blue emitting indium based MOF (ZJU-28), as shown in Fig. 5 a, was constructed with indium as the metal center [70]. What is interesting is that the fluorescent color of the CsPbX3@ZJU-28 nanocomposite can be tuned from violet to red by controlling the halide composition of the CsPbX3 QDs. Instead of luminescent metal center, Qin and Yan in their research successfully co-doped red emissive Eu3+ metal ions and blue CDs into the non-fluorescent MIL-53, thus forming dual emissive Eu3+/CDs@MIL-53 nanocomposite [67]. Besides using single QDs, different types of QDs can be encapsulated together into the porous MOFs for development of QD@MOF nanocomposites with dual emission properties. In Shokri's research, boron-doped CDs (B-CDs) and phosphorus-doped CDs (P-CDs) were successfully encapsulated into ZIF-8 nanocrystals, forming B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite that exhibited blue and green fluorescence that originated from the B-CDs and P-CDs, respectively [77]. Upon excitation at wavelength of 385 nm, the B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite demonstrated dual emission at 440 and 510 nm, similar to the mixture of B-CDs and P-CDs.

Apart from the fluorescence that originates from the QDs and MOFs, the luminescent guest molecules such as dyes and metal nanoclusters also play a significant role in the construction of multi-luminescent QD@MOF nanocomposite. Additional loading of emissive moieties into QD@MOF nanocomposite enhances its fluorescence performance for specific and accurate sensing of desired analytes. The fluorescence dyes, which are composed of organic molecules with high PL quantum yield, are considered as readily obtainable luminescent guests to be incorporated with the QD@MOF nanocomposite [49,56,84]. For example, Li et al. successfully encapsulated rhodamine 6G (R6G) luminous dye and cadmium sulfide QDs into porous ZIF-8, which exhibited blue and red emission under UV excitation, as shown in Fig. 5b [82]. Besides, metal nanoclusters, which are known as ultrasmall nanoparticles (<2 nm), consist of a few or hundreds of metal atoms that have strong optical characteristics and good photostability [83,85,86]. In 2020, Jiang et al. developed double emissive AgNCs/BPQDs/MOF nanocomposites by embedding blue emitting BPQDs and red emitting gold nanoclusters into the nanochannels of non-fluorescent ZIF-8, proven by the PL emission spectra in Fig. 5c [80].

The change in PL signals of the QD@MOF after the addition of target analytes is considered as the response. The main PL responses of QD@MOF as nanosensors are PL enhancement (turn on), PL quenching (turn off), and ratiometric response, as shown in Fig. 6 . As turn-on sensors, QD@MOFs cause an enhancement in PL intensity upon the addition of specific analytes. The enhancement of PL results from crosslinking effects, where covalent or non-covalent bonds form between QD@MOFs and the analytes [87]. These bonds create rigidity and prevent non-radiative transitions. Examples of chemical or physical crosslinking that can lead to luminescence amplification include complexation with ions and interactions within confined domains. In contrast, for turn-off sensors, the addition of target analytes leads to PL quenching. Collisional quenching occurs when the excited-state QD@MOF (fluorophore) is deactivated upon contact with target analytes (quenchers) to result in charge or energy transfer [88]. The excited-state lifetime of a fluorophore decreases with increasing quenchers concentration regardless of the absorption spectral profile of the fluorophore. Generally, the PL turn off and PL turn on responses can be observed in singular emissive QD@MOF nanocomposite. Meanwhile, dual emissive QD@MOF nanocomposite can be used as ratiometric sensors. This is because the two emission bands from different centers offer different efficacy in the presence of the target. The types of responses of the QD@MOF toward various analytes such as metal ions, explosives, water, temperature and biological components (proteins, vitamins, quercetin, etc.) are presented in Table 3 together with the achieved detection parameters such as response range, and LOD.

In general, there is an impression that the response from QD@MOF composite with one PL emission center is good enough for quantitative analysis, but some detection characteristics such as selectivity, sensitivity, linear range, and LOD are poor when combined with instrumental techniques. Compared to single emissive QD@MOF sensor, dual emissive QD@MOF with ratiometric response significantly improve the sensitivity and selectivity of target sensing by controlling the ratio of two PL emission bands in absence and presence of analytes [36]. Wider applications for the QD@MOFs are made possible by such ratiometric fluorescence sensors with numerous signal outputs, which are potential platforms for naked-eye field measurements [112]. The development of novel QD@MOF with impressive performance by integrating multi-PL emission and ratiometric response would be a promising trend in the field of fluorescence sensors for the future.

The luminescent QD@MOF sensors are designed for selective recognition of analytes based on the interactions between the receptor and the target. The QDs, MOFs, and other luminous guests, which are the common receptors involved in sensing pathways of QD@MOF nanosensors, can also act as co-receptors when combined with MOFs or additional luminous guests. When the target interacts with the receptor, a specific response based on possible mechanisms is detected, indicating the binding of the analyte which is responsible for the sensing performance. Based on review of the literature, the mechanisms of fluorescent QD@MOF sensors are mainly divided into four types as shown in Fig. 7 :

The overlap between the absorption spectrum of the target analytes and the PL excitation or emission spectrum of the sensor would result either in turn on, turn off or ratiometric response. The PL excitation and emission spectra of QD@MOF with the combination of UV–Vis absorption spectra of the target analyte is essential to investigate the possibility of an overlap mechanism. Using QDs as target receptor, Xu et al. in their research successfully employed CDs@ZIF-8 for highly selective and sensitive detection of quercetin (QCT) in a wide concentration range of 0.01–50 μM [107]. The addition of CDs@ZIF-8 in the QCT solution resulted in the emergence of a new shoulder UV absorption band of 410 nm–530 nm, which partially overlaps the fluorescence emission range of CDs in ZIF-8 (Fig. 8 a). This spectral overlap is attributed to the energy transfer between the 3-hydroxyl group of QCT and the basic groups present on the surface of the CDs; which in turn, this leads to a sensitive and selective quenching of the fluorescence emitted by the CDs.

The interaction between the functional group of QD@MOF and the target analyte causes changes in flexibility, conformation, or rigidity of the functional group, which leads to the generation of both turn off and turn on responses. In a study conducted by Tan et al., a dual emissive CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was proposed to detect Cu2+ [81]. In this study, the AuNC served as the receptor, where the Cu2+ could coordinate with the –COOH of GSH at the surface of AuNCs, thus quenching the fluorescence of the AuNCs, as illustrated in Fig. 8b. Based on this principle, the PL of the AuNCs was turned off and the PL of the CDs was used as a reference, enabling ratiometric fluorescence Cu2+ sensing, with a LOD of 0.3324 nM.

The analyte cations might diffuse into the pores and then be replaced with cationic metal ions inside the framework where such structural changes lead to a specific type of response toward a specific analyte. Hao et al. designed and synthesized CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs that exhibited ratiometric fluorescent response toward Cu2+ ions in a wide linear range of 50 nM to 10 mM [66]. The blue emission of the CDs was not affected and was used as a reference signal; however, the red emission of Eu-DPA MOF was greatly quenched when Cu2+ ions were added. The detection mechanism as proposed by the authors relies on the cation exchange interactions between Cu2+ ions and Eu3+ ions. The Cu2+ ions would replace the Eu3+ ions anchored to the nitrogen groups of DPA because of its stronger coordination ability with nitrogen atom of DPA than Eu3+. Such interaction results in fluorescent quenching of MOF, which implies that the receptor for the Cu2+ ions was MOFs instead of CDs.

The framework/structure of MOF (receptor) might collapse during the sensing process, causing a change in its fluorescence properties. Zhao et al. reported a framework collapse mechanism for sensing of anionic pollutant phosphate (Pi) with detection limit of up to 53 nM [95]. Encapsulation of positively charged ZnO QDs and negatively charged MOFs caused strong Zn-amine bonding and electrostatic interaction, resulting in luminescence quenching of ZnO QDs caused by the electron-transfer processes. Upon introduction of the Pi ions, the structure of the MOFs collapsed, which led to the release of ZnO QDs and the recovery of the fluorescence (Fig. 8c). Although the reported detection system was satisfactorily used for Pi assessment in environmental samples, a major drawback is that the probe developed based on the framework-collapse mechanism is not recyclable and stable.

Additionally, Wu and Yan developed a ratiometric Cu2+ ions sensor by incorporating red R-BF-CQDs with blue UiO-66-(COOH)2, where the sensor adopted a synergistic sensing pathway using QDs and MOFs as the co-receptors [89]. The Cu2+ ions bridged between the QDs and MOFs simultaneously (Fig. 8d) because of the presence of amine groups and carboxylate ions on the surface of R-BF-CQDs and UiO-66-(COOH)2, respectively. This induced the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from UiO-66-(COOH)2 to the R-BF-CQDs, leading to spectral overlap between the absorption band of Cu2+-R-BF-CQDs complex and the emission band of MOF. Through the above mechanisms, the addition of Cu2+ caused a decrease in the blue PL of the MOF and an increase in the red PL of the QD, which improved the ratiometric fluorescent sensor's ability to detect Cu2+ at a detection limit of 20 nM. In this case, the overlap mechanism along with the functional group interaction mechanism were utilized for sensitive detection of Cu2+. The details about the recognized receptor-mechanism for detection of various analytes by QD@MOF are further discussed in Table 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced performance of QDs due to encapsulation in MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: The unique properties of QDs, combined with the well-defined porous structures of MOFs, result in enhanced performance in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, colorimetric response, stability, recyclability, and real-world sensing applications. The encapsulation of QDs within MOFs offers opportunities for tailoring the sensor properties based on specific analyte requirements. By designing the MOF structure and surface functionalities, researchers can achieve high affinity and specificity toward the target molecules, resulting in improved sensing performance.

The integration of QDs and MOFs allows for the development of "on-off" or ratiometric sensing approaches, enabling sensitive detection and quantification of analytes at low concentrations. The enhanced sensitivity and selectivity of QD sensors encapsulated in MOFs have been demonstrated in various studies. Interestingly, Fan et al. fabricated a fluorescence “on-off” QD@MOF nanocomposite for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ [93]. The luminescence was turned on when the CQDs were encapsulated within the MOFs and the fluorescence quenching, resulting in an “off” state, was observed upon addition of the Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions. This happened because the metal ions strongly coordinated with the organic ligands of the MOFs, weakening the hydrogen bonds between the MOFs and CQDs, resulting in the CQDs being isolated from the MOFs. According to the authors, the sensing approach utilizing this type of “on-off” probe is more sensitive than the method using bare CQDs, with a LOD of 2.3 ppb and 1.3 ppb for Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively.

In another study, Lin et al. developed BPEI-CQDs/ZIF-8 for detection of Cu2+ ions with a very low LOD (80 pM) compared to other reported values for detection limit of Cu2+ ions [2]. The bright blue emission intensity of BPEI-CQDs/ZIF-8 was reduced when a wide range of Cu2+ ions (2−1000 nM) was added to the solution. The amino groups on the surface of the BPEI-CQDs interacted with the Cu2+ ions, resulting in a PL-inactive but UV-active compound, which triggered the turn off response. The excitation spectra of the CQDs in the MOFs were partially overlaid by a new shoulder UV adsorption associated with an amine copper group that was PL-inactive between 270 and 400 nm (Fig. 9 a), which resulted in fluorescence quenching in the BPEI-CQDs/ZIF-8 emission peak. The ZIF-8 counterparts’ cation adsorption behavior triggered the accumulation of Cu2+ ions around the nanocomposite, increasing both selectivity and sensitivity of detection.

The detection of nitroaromatic compounds (NACs) such as 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) is a huge challenge due to their high boiling point and limited chemical reactivity [113]. However, by exploiting the electron deficiency of these NACs, stable donor-acceptor charge-transfer complexes can be designed using electron rich QD@MOF, where the presence of the NACs can be quantified by observing the PL quenching or PL enhancement. For example, Devi et al. reported a turn off chemical sensor IRMOF-3/NCQD for highly selective detection of TNT with a low LOD of 17.07 ppb [99]. The fluorescence response mechanism can be construed as the formation of Jackson-Meisenheimer (JM) complex between the TNT and IRMOF-3, prompting spectral overlap between the JM complex absorption spectrum and the emission spectrum of IRMOF-3 particles, thus resulting in the fluorescence quenching phenomenon. Additionally, in another research, Yang and Kou strategically immobilized amino-CQDs into the pores of MIL-101-SO3H for an enhanced adsorption capacity and driving force for adsorption of DNP into the pore matrix through hydrogen bonding between the –NO2 groups of DNP and –SO3H groups of MIL-101-SO3H [101]. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the DNP overlapped partly with the PL emission spectrum of amino-CQDs, which resulted in greater PL quenching efficiency as the concentration of the DNP increased. This strategy allowed for a highly selective and sensitive detection of trace DNP in the concentration range of 0.01–40.0 μM, with a detection limit of 4.5 nM.

Pyrraline (PRL) is an advanced glycation end product that could accumulate in the human body and cause aging and some serious chronic diseases. Liu et al. [103] demonstrated a turn off sensor by anchoring the MIPs onto the surfaces of CdSe/ZnS QDs and MIL-101, with ultrasensitive sensing for PRL as low as 3.9 μmol L−1. This detection is based on transfer of charges in the QD conduction bands to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the PRL, which leads to decreased PL intensity. The authors claimed that high sensitivity was derived from the double signal amplification by the QDs and MOFs as an imprinting cavity. The drug 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP) is widely known as a cytotoxic anti-cancer drug, and thus, studying the mode by which it exerts its detection of cancer cells is worth doing. Jin et al. first reported an effective dual emission nanosensor by encapsulating green emissive MPA-CdTe QDs inside blue emissive NH2-MIL-53(Al) MOFs [104]. Due to the strong absorption of 6-MP, the excitation light of NH2-MIL-53(Al) was attenuated, which is attributed to the fluorescence IFE. Meanwhile, improved QDs passivation and increased PL intensity could be obtained from the complexation of cadmium ions on the QD surface by the thiol group of 6-MP. The descriptions above are the basics to establish the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of 6-MP with a LOD of 0.15 μM. More importantly, the researchers claimed that this novel sensor has higher selectivity toward 6-MP than CdTe QDs alone and higher sensitivity than bare MOFs.

Reported studies revealed that luminescent QD@MOF has high potential to contribute as fluorescence temperature sensor due to the integration of thermally responsive QDs/MOFs luminescence and the anti-aggregation effect from MOFs. For instance, Feng et al. established CQDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 by functionalizing CQDs on MOFs without aggregation, which resulted in better temperature sensing, with sensitivity up to 1.3 % K−1 [96]. The emission intensity of the CQDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 film decreased when the temperature rises from 97 to 297 K because the nonradiative decay pathways are thermally activated. Moreover, the researchers reported that the prepared film thermometer is reusable thrice without loss in PL intensity. In another study, Yao et al. reported that the PL intensity of the synthesized CDs@UiO-66(OH)2 quenched significantly at wavelength of 464 nm as the temperature increased from 25 to 110 °C [60]. More importantly, the composite exhibited excellent reusability as a luminescent sensor, where the luminescent switching process of between 30 and 95 °C could be repeated for at least five consecutive cycles. Similarly, Liu et al. rationally designed a self-calibrated ratiometric nanothermometer from CsPbBr3 QD encapsulated lanthanide MOFs (Eu-BTC), in which the CsPbBr3 QDs exhibited a thermoquenching reaction, while the Eu3+ ions in the Eu-BTC displayed thermo-enhancing luminescence property upon temperature increase from 20 to 100 °C [97]. The contrasting temperature sensing behavior of CsPbBr3 QDs and skeleton Eu3+ ions will be beneficial for the application of an optical nanothermometer that operates based on the PL intensity ratio of Eu3+ ions (618 nm) to CsPbBr3 (528 nm). In addition, the prepared novel CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC thermometer has a very high relative sensitivity of 3.9 % °C−1 and good cyclic detection stability.

The colorimetric response has emerged as a widely employed strategy in luminescent sensing applications. This approach offers several distinct advantages that make it desirable for various sensing scenarios. Unlike complex instrumentation or sophisticated detection methods, colorimetric sensing relies on the naked eye's perception of color changes, enabling simple and rapid detection without the need for specialized equipment. The observable color variations provide an intuitive and straightforward readout, facilitating user-friendly and on-site analysis. The combination of visual interpretation, simplicity, and reliable performance makes QD@MOF an appealing approach in luminescent sensing, opening new possibilities for applications in environmental monitoring, healthcare diagnostics, and beyond. For instance, Xu and Yan in their research designed and synthesized dual emissive Eu3+/CDs@MOF-253 composite as a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detection of Hg2+ with a broad response range of 0–150 mM and extremely low LOD of 13 nM [64]. The fluorescence of the CDs was quenched in the presence of Hg2+, while the fluorescence of the MOFs was unaffected, due to the specific interaction of the Hg2+ with several functional groups on the surface of the CDs. Additionally, the composite exhibited a specific and observable colorimetric response toward Hg2+, where the solution turned red when exposed to UV light as the concentration increased from 0 to 100 mM. Similarly, Guo et al. reported a dual emissive QD@MOF by encapsulating CDs and AuNCs inside ZIF-8, which can be applied as a colorimetric and ratiometric probe for Hg2+ detection [79]. The Hg2+ quenched the red fluorescence of the AuNCs due to the strong Hg2+-Au+ metallophilic interaction with no obvious change in the blue fluorescence of the CDs, indicating that the CDs did not interact with the Hg2+. Additionally, the detection of the Hg2+ could be easily visualized by the naked eye as the composite changed color from red to blue with increasing Hg2+ upon light irradiation. The LOD of this probe attained 1 nM, which is lower than the previously reported LOD by Xu and Yan [64]. Both the published papers suggest that highly efficient Hg2+ ions detection by QD@MOF composite can be achieved through strong target-linker interactions. Furthermore, proper selection of the embedded guest molecules within the framework of QD@MOF could enhance the sensitivity of the sensor. However, the leaching of these sensor types needs to be well studied for practical applications.

Intriguingly, Yi and Zhang designed a novel dual responsive colorimetric sensor for Pb2+ (turn off) and PO4 3− (turn on) by encapsulating CDs and thioglycolic acid capped CdTe QDs into porous ZIF-8 [75]. With CdTe QDs as the detection signal and CDs as the standard (Fig. 9b), the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 luminescence changed from red to blue upon the addition of Pb2+ ions. The exhibited quenching was due to the binding of Pb2+ with sulfhydryl group of thioglycolic acid on the surface of the QDs, which reduced the PL lifetime of the QDs. The successive addition of PO4 3− in the system destroyed the sulfhydryl- Pb2+ bonding and recovered the emission color from blue to red. The authors successfully prepared test papers for colorimetry-based quantification of Pb2+ and PO4 3− in actual samples utilizing the PL “on-off-on” approach, without needing the instrument-specific calibration.

In various industrial processes that involve organic solvents, water is one of the most common contaminants. Hence, for sensitive industrial applications, it is important to develop simple and reliable sensors for determination of water impurities in organic solvents [114]. For instance, Dong et al. in their study synthesized strong blue emitting nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) which were entrapped in red emissive Eu-MOFs, where this combination was applied as a colorimetric and ratiometric sensor for water contents in ethanol with a broad response range of 0.2 %–30 % (Fig. 9c) [63]. As the percentage of water in ethanol gradually increased, the emission from Eu-MOFs was turned off while the emission from N,S-CDs was turned on. This occurred because of the O–H oscillator effect on the luminescence of the Eu3+ complexes. More significantly, the apparent red to blue color shifts caused by the simultaneous PL changes provides for a sensitive and visual detection technique for water in ethanol with a low LOD of 0.03 %. A new nanocomposite material that combined Mn2+: ZnS QDs and ZIF-8 for Co2+ and Cr2O7 2− detection, with a response range of 0–100 μM, was first proposed in a study by Ref. [90]. The turn off response was observed with a visual change from orange red to colorless when Co2+ or Cr2O7 2− was added in the detection system upon radiation at 254 nm. For Co2+ detection mechanism, the authors suggested that the quenching of the composite may be due to some interactions between Co2+ and functional groups of the Mn2+: ZnS QDs. Meanwhile, for evaluation of Cr2O7 2−, the FRET mechanism occurred when the absorption peak of Cr2O7 2− overlapped with the excitation peak of the composite. Additionally, Fu et al. successfully established a dual response ratiometric fluorescence sensor by combining CdTe QDs, CDs@ZIF-8, and MIP for the fast 4-NP detection in the two concentration ranges of 0.05 μM–30 μM and 0.1 pM–3.0 pM with the LOD of 0.05 μМ and 0.08 pM, respectively [78]. The emission peaks at 420 nm and 703 nm of the nanocomposite quenched as the concentration of the 4-NP increased (Fig. 9d) due to the electron transfer between 4-NP and the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP, in which the hydroxyl and nitro groups of 4-NP interacted with the amino groups in the binding site of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP. Moreover, the variation of the fluorescence intensity ratio caused a change in the color of the fluorescence from purple to pink to red, enabling the visual detection of 4-NP in real environment with satisfactory recoveries (97.5–106.3 %).

The encapsulation of QDs within MOFs has emerged as a promising strategy for enhancing the stability of these luminescent nanomaterials. The inherent fragility of QDs, particularly in terms of thermal stability and photo stability, has limited their practical applications. However, the incorporation of QDs into MOF matrices provides a protective shield, effectively shielding the QDs from harsh environmental conditions. This encapsulation imparts remarkable thermal stability, enabling the QDs to withstand elevated temperatures without significant degradation. Moreover, the photo stability of QDs is greatly improved through MOF encapsulation, allowing them to retain their luminescent properties even under prolonged exposure to light. Additionally, the long-term storage stability of QDs is enhanced by the presence of MOFs, preventing their aggregation or degradation over extended periods. The combination of enhanced thermal stability, photo stability, and long-term storage stability makes QD@MOF composites highly attractive for various sensing applications.

For example, Kumagai et al. conducted a study highlighting the effectiveness of MOFs as straightforward protective materials for QDs [115]. To assess the suitability of ZIF-8 as a protective coating for QDs, the researchers introduced 1,4-benzoquinone, an effective electron acceptor known for its ability to quench the PL of CdSe/CdS core/shell QDs. They added 1,4-benzoquinone to a solution containing pyridine-capped CdSe/CdS core/shell QDs, as well as a suspension of CdSe/CdS QD@ZIF-8 composite. The results, shown in Fig. 10 a, demonstrated that 1,4-benzoquinone acted as a potent quencher for the pyridine-capped CdSe/CdS core/shell QDs, causing a significant reduction in PL intensity with increasing quencher concentration. However, when the CdSe/CdS QD@ZIF-8 composites were used instead, minimal PL quenching was observed even in the presence of 1,4-benzoquinone at the same concentration range. MOFs are well-known for their ability to absorb organic molecules into their cavities; however, the pore diameter of ZIF-8 is limited to approximately 0.3 nm. Since the size of the 1,4-benzoquinone molecule exceeded the cavity size of ZIF-8, it could not penetrate the MOF's pores. Consequently, the encapsulated QDs remained unaffected by the added quenchers.

The stability of CH3NH3PbX3 PQDs is poor as they can easily decompose in the presence of water and oxygen in the air. To solve this issue, Zhang et al. grew nano-sized CH3NH3PbBr3 PQDs in situ within micro-sized MOFs (Bio-MOF-1) to create PQDs-Bio-MOF-1 composites that are protected by MOFs. These composites not only retained their optical properties such as strong fluorescence and a narrow emission spectrum, but also showed significantly improved air-stability. To compare the stability of PQDs-Bio-MOF-1 to unprotected CH3NH3PbBr3 PQDs, the researchers conducted several experiments. The results showed that CH3NH3PbBr3 PQD powder lost its fluorescence immediately after toluene evaporated completely in air, whereas PQDs-Bio-MOF powder maintained good fluorescence even after 90 days of air exposure. These findings indicate that the stability of PQDs-Bio-MOF-1 in air is greatly enhanced compared to CH3NH3PbBr3 PQDs [116]. Similarly, Ren et al. in their research compared the thermal stability, photo stability, and long-term storage stability of CsPbBr3@ZJU-28 with CsPbBr3 powders [70]. Apart from its outstanding PL properties, the ZJU-28 framework exhibited remarkable structural stability, making it an ideal candidate for use as a protective shell in CsPbBr3@ZJU-28. The thermal stability test revealed that the CsPbBr3@ZJU-28 retained 64.7 % of its original PL intensity at 80 °C, while the CsPbBr3 powders almost disappeared, and even at 160 °C, the CsPbBr3@ZJU-28 still emitted light. To demonstrate its photo-stability, the CsPbBr3@ZJU-28 and CsPbBr3 powders were placed 10 cm below a 365 nm UV lamp (48 W) in ambient atmosphere. The CsPbBr3@ZJU-28 retained 84.5 % of its original PL intensity after being exposed to the UV lamp for 80 h. The long-term storage stability of the CsPbBr3@ZJU-28 was also significantly improved, as it retained 83.5 % of its PL intensity after being stored at ambient atmosphere (with an average temperature of 20 °C and average humidity of 15 %) for 60 days.

High recyclability is a crucial attribute that significantly enhances the efficiency and sustainability of luminescent sensing applications. The QD@MOF sensing materials with excellent recyclability can be regenerated and restored to their original state after each sensing cycle, allowing for multiple uses without compromising their performance. By enabling repeated sensing cycles without sacrificing sensitivity or selectivity, the high recyclability of luminescent sensing platforms enhances the longevity and cost-effectiveness of the platforms, making them valuable tools for long-term monitoring and analysis.

Zhang et al. reported the synthesis of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 for sensitive, selective, and recyclable detection of Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− in aqueous environments over a wide linearity region with a LOD of 0.16 μM for Cr2O7 2− and 0.17 μM for CrO4 2− [91]. The IFE from the Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− ions was able to shield the excitation spectra of the SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, leading to a quenching effect of QDs which could be distinguished by the naked eye. The authors proved high recyclability of the developed sensor (Fig. 10b), which could be regenerated after detection by rinsing with alcohol and reused for at least five cycles. In another study, Zhang et al. explored the utility of fluorescent CH3NH3PbBr3 perovskite QDs embedded inside MOF-5 for stable temperature sensing where they reported the highest recyclable response range of 30–230 °C compared to other reported investigations [92]. The linear relationship between logarithmic fluorescence intensity and temperature is exhibited with the attainment of a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9944, allowing the CH3NH3PbBr3@MOF-5 nanocomposites to work as a turn off luminous thermometer. Interestingly, this research group also demonstrated that the nanocomposite is reusable for up to seven cycles, and the PL intensity of the nanocomposite can be recovered with decreasing temperature of 230 to 30 °C, indicating that there is a reversible process depending on the heating and cooling temperature. Similarly, Yang et al. successfully assembled recyclable CdTe QDs@ZIF-8 composite for ratiometric fluorescence analysis of CTC with a quick response time (1 min), where the calculated LOD was 37 nM [105]. Here, the spectral overlap between the absorption of CTC and excitation spectra of the nanocomposite caused IFE, resulting in PL quenching at emission peak of 672 nm, while strong coordination between Zn2+ in ZIF-8 and CTC caused the excited intramolecular proton transfer of CTC, thus inducing amplified emission at 521 nm. Noteworthily, the authors further fabricated the nanocomposite into test strips which exhibited excellent recyclability of up to 6 cycles, where the color of the strips transformed from red to green by visual inspection.

The translation of laboratory-scale innovations to real-world applications is a critical step in the development of sensing technologies. While the performance of QD@MOF sensor composites can be extensively characterized and optimized in controlled laboratory settings, their true value lies in their ability to deliver reliable results in practical and diverse sensing environments. Real-world sensing tests serve as essential validation tools, allowing for the evaluation of sensor performance under realistic conditions, such as varying analyte concentrations, complex sample matrices, and fluctuating environmental factors. By subjecting QD@MOF sensor composites to these challenging scenarios, the effectiveness and robustness of the sensor can be assessed, providing insights into its accuracy, sensitivity, selectivity, and durability. Real-world sensing tests enable researchers and developers to identify potential limitations, optimize the sensor's design and operation, and ultimately ensure its suitability for real-world applications. For instance, Wang et al. composed a novel blue luminescence cobalt and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots encapsulated in europium MOF (CDs@Eu-MOFs) for sensitive ratiometric detection of Cr2O7 2− ions in real samples from the tap river, the Yellow River and steam [69]. The PL intensity of CDs (λem = 467 nm) decreased as the Cr2O7 2− ions increased, while the emission of Eu-MOF (λem = 616 nm) remained unchanged (Fig. 10c). The response mechanism can be interpreted as an overlap between Cr2O7 2− absorption band and the CDs' excitation band, thus resulting in PL quenching based on the IFE. Hence, the presence of Cr2O7 2− ions in environmental samples can be analyzed quickly and effectively using CDs as a detection signal and Eu-MOF as the reference standard, overcoming the environmental interference from onsite factors like pH, temperature, and others.

Abnormal levels of ascorbic acid (AA) (>110 μM) are greatly linked to many diseases such as stomach cramps, urinary stones, and diarrhea. In their research, Chen et al. synthesized a stable complex using CdTe QDs and UiO-66-NH2 with a low LOD (39.5 μM), which was successfully applied for the detection of AA in human serum samples, establishing the application of the UiO-66-2@CdTe in a complex environment [61]. The UiO-66-NH2@CdTe sensor acted as a ratiometric probe for selective sensing of AA based on UiO-66-NH2 and CdTe electronic transfer mechanism. Upon addition of AA, the emission from CdTe increased while the UiO-66-NH2 emission was quenched. One possible response mechanism could be that CdTe acts as the electron donor for the UiO-66-NH2 while AA serves as a reducing agent that forms a connection with UiO-66-NH2 through hydrogen bonding. Additionally, Tan et al. employed a CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe to analyze Cu2+ in serum samples using the standard addition method [81]. The original Cu2+ concentrations in the three human serum samples were 0.13, 0.18, and 0.16 μM, respectively, falling within the normal range of 11.00–24.39 μM without requiring dilution. Subsequently, Cu2+ with different concentrations (20 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM) was added to each serum sample. The recovery of Cu2+ ranged from 95.49 % to 104.4 %, and the relative standard deviation ranged from 2.1 % to 4.5 %. These results indicate the precision and accuracy of CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 in determining Cu2+ levels in real samples.

In another study, the presence of Cu2+ caused the strong luminescence emitted by CD@UiO-66-NH2 to diminish due to the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex, CD@UiO-66-NH2- Cu2+ [94]. However, when quinalphos is introduced into the sensing medium, the fluorescence intensity is restored because quinalphos has an affinity for Cu2+, making it a reliable detection method for quinalphos. In a practical study aimed at confirming the sensor's viability, a sensing experiment was conducted using two different agricultural products, namely tomatoes and rice extracts. These extracts were spiked with varying concentrations of quinalphos (0.9, 1.4, and 1.9 μg/mL) solutions. The recovery percentage ranged from 98 % to 105 %, indicating that this fluorescence sensor system exhibits reasonable accuracy and can be employed for on-field quinalphos detection in real-world samples.

Xu et al. developed a sensor material, CDs@ZIF-8, which exhibited desirable PL provided by the CDs and used the external porosity of the ZIF-8 for selective absorption and sensitive detection of QCT in aqueous solution over a wide concentration range of 0.01–50.0 μM [107]. The spectral overlap between the QCT absorption band and the CD excitation band was due to electrostatic interaction between the basic groups of CDs and 3-hydroxyl of QCT, resulting in the PL turn off response. The researchers further exploited the evaluation of QCT in Ginkgo biloba extract capsules with lower LOD (2.9 nM) by imprinting the MIP into the CDs@ZIF-8 system. The practical use of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor was tested by measuring the quantity of QCT in Ginkgo biloba extract capsules from five different manufacturers. The measured QCT levels were similar to the results obtained using the conventional HPLC-UV method, thus demonstrating that the developed sensor is accurate, reliable, and suitable for detecting QCT in real samples. In another study, the practical potential of using CdTe QDs@ZIF-8 for CTC detection in basa fish and pure milk was demonstrated [105]. The sensing mechanism functions through the inner filtration effect of CTC, leading to the quenching of the red emission from CdTe QDs. Simultaneously, the presence of Zn2+ in ZIF-8 triggered the sensitization effect, resulting in the appearance of green emission from the CTC as illustrated in Fig. 10d. The recovery rates of CTC spiked in the pure milk and basa fish were found to be within the ranges of 91.0–110.0 % and 95.0–106.0 %, respectively. Additionally, a comparison was made with the gold standard method of HPLC, which is used to measure CTC in pure milk. The response of CdTe QDs@ZIF-8 toward the CTC reached an equilibrium within 1 min, while the retention time of CTC during HPLC analysis was approximately 15 min. Therefore, CdTe QDs@ZIF-8 offers a shorter time of analysis compared to HPLC, exhibiting clear advantages over the gold standard method of HPLC.

Recent research progresses concerning the well-established synthesis methods and PL principles of QD@MOF composites have been summarized. Furthermore, we have demonstrated the successful application of QD@MOF as potential candidates for luminescent sensing in various chemical and biological systems, considering factors such as signal, response, receptors, and mechanisms. This approach has resulted in an effective sensing strategy with acceptable detection parameters. We have also briefly discussed the extensive achievements made by researchers in enhancing sensitivity, selectivity, LOD, real-time detection capabilities, and overall performance of QD@MOF fluorescence sensors.

Although numerous advances and innovations have been witnessed, QD@MOF sensors are still in the infant stage and some challenges remain to be solved. For instance, from the perspective of synthesis, the accessibility of the encapsulated QDs by the targets has not been well explored, despite enormous efforts devoted to developing strategies for embedding QDs within MOFs. It is crucial to ensure the pore size, properties of organic ligand, and metal node of MOFs are well matched with the size, intrinsic properties, and surface charge of the QDs in order to enhance the synergistic effect and host−guest interaction of QD@MOF. Furthermore, the interactions at the interfaces between QDs and MOFs remarkably influence the performance of QD@MOF composites. Therefore, an in-depth study to understand these interactions is highly desirable to indicate the structure–property relationships of QD@MOF. In terms of sensing, the development of a multidimensional sensing technique utilizing intensity, lifetime, wavelength, and polarization may open up a new direction to boost the QD@MOF sensing accuracy and reliability. In the pursuit of designing luminescent QD@MOF materials with high stability for real-time applications, increased efforts should be dedicated to maintaining their exceptional detection performance in various complex systems. This effort is essential to mitigate potential interference from factors such as aqueous conditions, temperature variations, pressure fluctuations, and mechanical stress. The structural stability of QD@MOF can be assessed using multiple characterization techniques, including SEM for morphology, PXRD for structural integrity, TGA for thermal stability, FTIR for functionality, and ICP for leaching behavior. Furthermore, comprehensive investigations into toxicology are required to ensure the biocompatibility of luminescent QD@MOF materials. Combining these diverse stability assessments will further advance the development of QD@MOF nanocomposites for chemical and biological sensing. Concurrently, addressing the practical implementation of QD@MOF as a functional probe is an urgent priority that needs immediate attention. This is crucial to elevate its performance to a level suitable for real-world applications. Presently, the development of QD@MOF as thin films, membranes, or hydrogels for practical use remains relatively rare, and there exists a notable knowledge gap in this area that requires attention.

The rapid development of QD@MOF composites in recent years has opened up numerous opportunities for the versatile design of luminescent sensing nanocomposites, with the potential for industrialization and commercialization in the near future. We anticipate that this review will be a valuable resource for the scientific community actively pursuing innovative modifications to enhance existing QD@MOF-based luminescent sensors for a wide range of applications.

Thivyah Balakrishnan: Writing – Original Draft, Visualization Wei Lun Ang: Conceptualization, Writing – Review & Editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition Ebrahim Mahmoudi: Data Curation, Visualization Nonni Soraya Sambudi: Validation, Writing – Review & Editing.
